speaker: Rob Moore
words: There was some sort of feuding between the Catholics and the Lutherans up on the northern end of the ridge.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: A victory?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Mm -hmm. It wasn't violent or anything, but there was some bad feeling. It could have been the form of our time. Yeah, we never heard of it. I won't say it couldn't have happened. No, it could have. You ought to talk to some of those old guys around farther. I don't know whether any of them was alive or not, but there was a murder or two up there one time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You remember?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: In that book you remember. Ann Riskelson. I remember hearing of that song when I was a kid of this, oh, somebody shot the sheriff. I can't remember the name. I don't know. But I remember hearing about it. I'll tell you who might be able to throw a little light on that. You might ask Marty House. It would have to be hearsay with Marty. Well, from his dad. Yeah. He's not too much younger than me. No, I know he isn't. Goresiesel lives up there now, but Cecil was raised right down here. Yeah.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I can't remember those names. How about things like during Prohibition, which of these towns around here, I know one time, Bowville was a closed town and Clarkie was open.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was Troy closed or open or Kendrick?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Closed.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: They were all closed?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah. I know I went up to Clarkie one time the 4th of July. I had no call living up there in this town. Oh, wait a minute. Yeah, it was Clarkie. Yeah. And booze was right on the table. They went and went in a, I don't know, I guess you'd call it a cafe at that time. And they were playing cards and there was booze on the table. And everything was closed up down here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did the town itself vote on whether they wanted to be open or closed?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, no, every state was missed. Local officers, they called it. Well, anyway, you see Bowville is in Latinell County. And Latinell County kept the sheriff there in Bowville. That's all the time. Although there was a lot of whiskey floating around when the sheriff was there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: That was Pat Malone, wasn't it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Hey, old Pat Malone ought to give you an old earful.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you ever talk to Pat? Is he still alive?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Hand in it, you're still alive. Well, it's the summer.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Pat Malone?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Pat Malone, yeah. He was a great big man. No.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, who was that?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Pat, he was a big man who was a little guy. Pat Moody. Pat Moody. Pat Moody. The Malone was big as a horse. Yeah, that's right. You know, Pat was one of those big old overcoatings. He always had a pint in it. Yeah. He could take care of most of the whiskey there was up there. No, Pat Moody, you're thinking of him. Pat Moody. Pat Moody, he always had the red carnation on him. Pat Moody. Yeah, red carnation.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He was up there for years in Bowville, wasn't he?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I don't know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Wasn't Pat Moody up there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He was a sheriff for years. No, he was definitely in there and he got to be sheriff. See, he was definitely under John. The summer feel, and then John, and then Moody. Yeah. No, Pat, Pat and Malone have been dead several years. Yeah, I got... He used to come down here at the dances when they'd have the dance moves by Pat and... I got two pats mixed up. Pat Moody, you're thinking about. Yeah.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: You're awful nervous this afternoon. Well, they used to here in prohibition, you know. You could always get a bottle of whiskey. We'd call a local option to get it down to drug or still. Dad would just get in there and get a bottle. I imagine a bottle of a pint of whiskey would last a damn for years. He'd get a medicine shelf and when he'd go on a little cough syrup, you know, a little pint of whiskey, he'd get a Jimmy Durban whiskey. Yeah. And he's the one that had on the bottle, don't buy booze. If your kids need shoes, you know that, Walt. Yeah. And Daddy went out here to Albert Mosscough to go just a minute. He'd get a pint of that. He always had a little around there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that made around here? Was that shipped in from somewhere?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, no, that was bonded whiskey. It wasn't moonshine. It was bonded whiskey. Oh, there was lots of moonshine floating into Kenrick, just where it came from, I don't know, but...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: It got here, isn't it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It came in. One time you could buy it without any trouble.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I was trying to think, whom the devil was it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, Archie May went into the drugstore, got a bottle of formaldehyde and took it across the street and put it in his buggy. In the back of his buggy and somebody saw him and he supposed to put whiskey in it. And so he went over and slipped the bottle out and took a big stirrup. Now, try, I can't think of who in the devil was it. You don't have to kill them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Who in the hell was that?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: That took the stirrup for a while.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I'll ask Frankie some day. He used to use it to treat the bleach in them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Huh?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I said he used to use it to treat the bleach in them. Yeah. If you got another few months in there, it would kill the bleach and it wouldn't germinate. I don't imagine the type of tape would want to swallow it. I don't know whether you could even swallow it or not. I think you could have it in your mouth. Burn my power.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Was this, um, up further north on the ridge and down in Troy and stuff, there was sometimes some hard feeling between the Swedish people and the Norwegians.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that a problem down in this area too?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: No, no problem at all. That's why they call it American Ridge down here. Never had no full future troubles at all here. Everybody got along fine. That's really remarkable because it seems like most of the places in the county had some problems. No trouble at all.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What's the best neighborhood you could ever ask to be in?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Before our time though, Bob, there was some trouble.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Old Travely Kettle was sitting around. Here's an honorable devil. That's right. There's rare images too. Oh yes. I was trying to think of one that...

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: There was a place and it was just up south 40. That's where we're next to the door, I believe. Some guy lived down there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Who was it? Was his name Hammer?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well, there was a man by the name Hammer that lived here, but I don't know where he lived. I remember Dad speaking of Hammer. Well... But I don't know where he lived at. I think that was the place. And he and Charlie were talking and he had to go to Moscow to prove upon his place to, you know, sign the final papers or something. I don't know just what the deal was. And Charlie convinced him there was no need for him to go, but he had to go and he'd take care of it. And he went out and he took care of it. But he wound up with the 40.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How could he do something like that?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I don't know. I just don't know. And whoever told me was in the know and I can't remember now who in the devil it was that told me that. They said he killed the old boy. He bruised over it and... Well, it sure didn't kill Charlie. No, it didn't hurt Charlie a bit.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: There's another one that old Charlie pulled.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: There's a quote from Goodwin.

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I know over on Little Bear there was a guy named McCoone over there. And everybody in the neighborhood kind of used to play tricks on him sometimes. I guess he was kind of a weird old sort. Like he was a bachelor and he built himself a huge five or six bedroom house. And there was a lot of interesting things happened between him and the other people on the ridge.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there people like that here too? Were some of the early homesteaders kind of odd sorts?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I don't recall any.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you, Bob? What were the early homesteaders like? Why did they come out here, do you think? What would make them pick up stakes somewhere and move out here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It was a promised land. Yeah, that's right. It was a promised land. Most of them come here with bachelors. Homesteaders proved up and if they had a family, they brought the family out a few years or they might have met some girl and got married. They were pretty much old. I remember coming here in the first... George Doherty and John Robbins, Sam Bigham, Frank May and Martin Thomas and all them. They were all bachelors.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: An old Frank a bachelor?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: When they first come here, I think he was. They were all bachelors. You see, John, John Roberts and Sam Bigham and George Doherty come here together. I remember the session week one time, Don Clarence was there and George come out and climbed up on my truck. And I said, Mr.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Doherty, I said, just when did you come here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He said, we come in the fall of 77. I remembered it was 101 years after the decoration of independence.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I said, who comes?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He says, Sam Bigham and John right up here and myself. They said, we settle right here, join each other.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do they know each other when they came?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh yeah, they come together. They come here together. They're three bachelors. And he says, we settle right here together. The reason I took this place here, it's all the steep ground, I took it on account of water. And Sam, he had a spring or whatever he settled in. Yeah, he had water. He had water and I don't know about John. John wasn't very far from George, but maybe he had a spring up there, I don't know. No, I don't. But George told me, he says, that's why I settle here, let me come to the water. There's water everywhere. They come here together. And then Martin, I don't know if Martin come a year or two after that and Frank May come the next fall. He said, we were here one year before Frank May. That wasn't exactly the way May had it when they had that piece. It wasn't exactly like what George had, but I think George had it right. George had it right. George was honest. There was an honest man living than George George. Then on one day, he said, we come the year before Frank May. Martin had some land over at Tennessee.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't he?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: That's right.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't he have a place over there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: His home said to go over there. It was between Genesee and Union House.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Then how did he sell that and buy this over here?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: He sold that and come over here.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Yeah?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: That's how he came over here. It was two or three years after George and Sam and John come. He sold his homestead. He come over and bought that land down there and he kept that on account of water. Water everywhere you looked there, you know. He got ooders of water and dirty, got plenty of water. And Bingham got plenty of water, but Bingham's water ran out. And drill a well. Water table was lower.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You remember, Bingham used to have water in the basement of the house, remember?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh yeah, he had a pump right in the kitchen. He had a pump right in the kitchen. Pump in the kitchen, pump right out of the basement. I don't know. The water level went down. An old picture pump, I remember that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you ever talk to Doherty or any of those folks about what the land was like and what the work was like and things? The first few years they were here, the kind of things they did?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: It was all bunchgrass prairies down where they were. There was bunchgrass here in the South slope, but these drawers all had timber in them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: So they didn't have to clear anything then?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Bunchgrass and road brush. That's right. And there was something else that was quite prevalent, was sunflowers. Oh yeah, sunflowers all over, growing in the bunchgrass. That too. Had a stalk as big as my wrist. Yeah, his dad told me, he says it took a good team to pull a flower through a sunflower stalk. And a great big, heavy cusp.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was you over there at the cemetery a number of years ago when we worked that cemetery over?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: And you remember somebody, I really thought of it, had one of these little John Deere two cylinder crawler green. Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You remember that?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I'll never forget that. We moved the stones all over, of course we had a plot at the cemetery, and then they'd go, there was trees in that bush up there 20 feet high, pull them and haul them down there south on the rock side hill. I remember there was a big old sunflower there, and hardly that sunflower stuck that little tractor here. It was set there in spin the tractor. It had a blade on it. And them things were tough. He had a little cloud before the two bottomed clouds. He was going to bulldoze it out with that blade. And he just sat in spin's track. Well them old sunflowers were tough. They were all over the south flinch in the bunch grass.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Bunch grass?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Uh -uh. For prairie.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was there a lot of sod? Did they have to break and turn the sod when they first started? Sod?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Yeah. Well I don't think bunch grass was very sodded. No, no. Just what it says, there's a bunch of grass.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Just a bunch here and a bunch there?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Just a bunch of blue grass. Mm -hmm. There was some bunches. Not so far apart. Yeah, they uh, I don't know, but they used three horses on a breaking plow. Three horses and a foot burner. I don't know how big the plow was, probably 14 inches. I think it was 14 inches. Yeah. Now no granddad or dad said that he broke all this place up except about 10 acres. Three Indian causas, these go down, Indian causas, and the Indians pretend all the peace. Broke it up with a foot burner and three causas.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Your dad broke up most of this place?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: I don't know. Yeah, I think he did. I think he broke this up too.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't, didn't, how did Gere, Gere have this before Frank Roberts got it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Well granddad bought it from Tom Gere.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, he bought it from Tom Gere? How gere is he proved up of it?

speaker: Norla Callison or Walter Benscoter
words: Oh, well. And then he left here and granddad bought him out. Well Gere, Gere built the barn. Yes. Because Frank told me that. Gere built the barn. Well this place must have been broke up then. It must have been, but I know that up there. Dad broke it all up by 10 acres. But Tom Gere.
